coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence and lifetime studies of C-dot decorated CdS/ZnFe2O4 composite designed for photoelectrochemical applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Kar, Archita
Dagar, Preeti
Kumar, Sandeep
Singh Deo, Ib
Vijaya Prakash, G.
Ganguli, Ashok Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium sulphide (CdS) is a well-known material for photoelectrochemical water splitting as a photoanode. The valence and conduction band edges are well suited for both hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions. However, a major drawback in the case of cadmium sulphide is its photocorrosion. In this regard, formation of a Type II heterostructure helps in well separation of the photogenerated charge carriers and reduces photocorrosion. Herein, we have incorporated a carbon dot layer (high conductivity) in between a CdS/ZnFe2O4 Type II composite heterostructure to further enhance the photoelectrochemical performance of the material. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) study revealed that maximum PL intensity is observed in the case of bare CdS while minimum intensity in the case of ternary composite after the incorporation of carbon dots. The inflation of PL intensity revealed the overall increase in the conductivity and enhanced charge separation at the interface after composite formation. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay dynamics have also been studied. The lifetime of the charge carriers for the ternary composite was found to be 2.41 ns, which is much higher than the bare (0.78 ns) and binary composite (0.85 ns), indicating the improved charge separation and the stability after composite formation (CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4). This further aids in the enhancement of photocurrent generation. The photocurrent density is enhanced by approximately 50 times compared to the bare material (CdS) and 5 times compared to the binary composite (CdS/ZnFe2O4).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium sulphide (CdS) is a well-known material for photoelectrochemical water splitting as a photoanode. The valence and conduction band edges are well suited for both hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions. However, a major drawback in the case of cadmium sulphide is its photocorrosion. In this regard, formation of a Type II heterostructure helps in well separation of the photogenerated charge carriers and reduces photocorrosion. Herein, we have incorporated a carbon dot layer (high conductivity) in between a CdS/ZnFe2O4 Type II composite heterostructure to further enhance the photoelectrochemical performance of the material. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) study revealed that maximum PL intensity is observed in the case of bare CdS while minimum intensity in the case of ternary composite after the incorporation of carbon dots. The inflation of PL intensity revealed the overall increase in the conductivity and enhanced charge separation at the interface after composite formation. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay dynamics have also been studied. The lifetime of the charge carriers for the ternary composite was found to be 2.41 ns, which is much higher than the bare (0.78 ns) and binary composite (0.85 ns), indicating the improved charge separation and the stability after composite formation (CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4). This further aids in the enhancement of photocurrent generation. The photocurrent density is enhanced by approximately 50 times compared to the bare material (CdS) and 5 times compared to the binary composite (CdS/ZnFe2O4).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Conversion of solar energy and its storage has become a very important part of research all over the world for a stable and sustainable future. In this regard, hydrogen energy is being considered as one of the cleanest forms of energy and hence is being under focus for the scientific community from a long time and it will be for at least another decade till it is fully commercialised[1]. Among various methods available for producing the clean fuel, the photoelectrochemical water splitting i.e. harnessing solar energy for the purpose of producing hydrogen and oxygen is considered as most cost effective and environment friendly pathway[2].

Semiconductors having band gap lying in the visible region can absorb maximum photons from the solar spectrum and help in utilisation of the abundant sunlight[3]. Among them, CdS has been extensively studied for its narrow band gap around 2.4 eV and its appropriate conduction and valence band edge positions for both hydrogen and oxygen production by water splitting [4–8]. However, the major drawback of using CdS nanoparticles is photo corrosion [9,10]. Hence, various methods like forming a heterojunction, coating with noble metals, loading with co-catalysts have been employed over time to prevent photocorrosion[11–14].

Spinels, more specifically ferrites have extra catalytic sites and have bandgap capable of absorbing visible light [15]. Among spinels, a p-type semiconductor ZnFe2O4 has grabbed significant attention in solar energy conversion due to its narrow band gap (1.9 eV), excellent efficiency in harvesting visible light and remarkable photostability [16–20]. ZnFe2O4 forms a Type II heterostructure with CdS which leads to separation of charge carriers. In 2013, Yu et al. have reported ZnFe2O4 decorated CdS nanorods for hydrogen production. The charge carriers are well separated by the migration of the photo-induced holes of CdS to photochemically more stable ZnFe2O4 as its valence band is higher than that of CdS and migration of electrons in the opposite direction [21]. As being discussed by L. Yao, et al. that in contrast to the binary heterostructure, ternary heterostructure photoelectrocatalysts improve the water splitting performance. This is due to gradual change of energy bands and efficient transfer of charge carriers as well as their efficient charge separation [4]. There are a few numbers of reports on the ternary heterostructures involving CdS and ZnFe2O4 [22–24].

Carbon based materials show very high electrical conductivity, good optical activity and good electrochemical stability[25–28]. Semiconductors with ultrathin carbon layers show excellent photoactivity and have been extensively studied in photocatalysis and battery applications [29–33]. The use of carbon dots for coating CdS nanoparticles have been studied first by Lou et al. [32], where they have mentioned that thin layers of carbon over the CdS particles can act as a shell which helps in efficient electron transfer and prevents the core materials from corrosion. The deposition of carbon layers over nanomaterials can be done by methods like pyrolysis of organic precursor [34], chemical vapour deposition [35]and simple hydrothermal method [33]. Many reports support the fact that amorphous carbon dots can also help in charge transfer process without creating trap states[36–38]. For example, Zhu et al. have synthesized amorphous carbon dots dispersed over TiO2-Cu as an electron mediator[39]. The good conductivity of the amorphous carbon dot layer helped in enhanced charge separation and hence boosting up the solar to hydrogen conversion process. Shi et al. have also reported that the introduction of carbon layer onto the photoelectrode results in improvement of electron transport and well separation of the charge carriers[37]. This is attributed to the resistance to corrosion and superior conductivity of the carbon layer.
Using C-dots and ZnFeO4 along with CdS will not only help in improvement of charge separation but will also enhance the conductivity of the system.

Steady state and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay studies further helps in understanding the mechanism of charge transfer in terms of their decay dynamics. The enhancement in electron-hole separation can be correlated with quenching in photoluminescence (PL) of the material. Y.Yang et al. have recently reported the role of nano- ZnS layer over CdS nanorods in the separation and transfer of charge carriers with the help of PL and TRPL [40]. Kang et al., have discussed extensively the optical properties of Ti0.91O2/CdS composite with the help of PL and TRPL studies [41].

Here, we have designed a ternary composite by the addition of a layer of carbon in the form of carbon dots, between CdS and ZnFe2O4. The prepared bare and composite materials have been well characterized and the optical properties have been investigated using UV–visible absorption, steady state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay studies which revealed information about the charge separation dynamics of the designed system. The photoelectrochemical properties of the ternary composite confirmed that the incorporation of C-dot layer have resulted in significant increase in the photocurrent density compared to the bare CdS and the binary composite, CdS/ZnFe2O4.

All chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Cadmium nitrate-4-hydrate (99.0 %, CDH chemicals), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, K-30, CDH chemicals), ferric chloride hexahydrate (98.0 %, CDH chemicals), thiourea (99.0 %, Indian Drugs and Pharmaceuticals), ethylene glycol (99.5 %, Qualigens), sodium acetate (99.0 %, Qualigens), ascorbic acid (99.7 %, SRL chemicals), anhydrous zinc chloride (95 %, Fischer Scientific), ethanol (≥99.8 %, Merck India) and Nafion (5 % w/w in water and isopropanol, Alfa Aesar).

CdS particles were synthesised by a modified solvothermal route reported earlier [42]. Initially, 0.1 M Cd(NO3).4H2O and 0.25 M of thiourea were dissolved in a 20:1 mixture of ethylene glycol and water and then stirred at room temperature for 15 min. Then 0.9 g of PVP was added and stirred for another 30 min. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 100 °C for 24 h. The yellow coloured precipitate obtained was washed with 1:1 mixture of ethanol and water several times and dried in an oven at 65 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnFe2O4, CdS/ZnFe2O4 and CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: ZnFe2O4 was synthesized by a hydrothermal route reported earlier [43]. For the synthesis purpose, 2 mmol ZnCl2, 4 mmol FeCl3·6H2O and 6 mmol NaAc were dissolved in 30 mL of ethylene glycol by stirring for around 30 min. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined autoclave, kept in oven and maintained at 200 °C for 24 h. The dark brown precipitate obtained was washed with ethanol and double distilled water several times and dried in an oven at 65 °C overnight. The synthesis of both CdS/ZnFe2O4 and CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 composites were carried out by the impregnation route [44]. For CdS-ZnFe2O4, 30 wt% of ZnFe2O4 was well dispersed in 1:1 mixture of ethanol and water and sonicated for 1 h. CdS was added to the above mixture and stirred magnetically for about 15 h. The product was washed with ethanol and double-distilled water and dried in an oven at 65 °C overnight. For the synthesis of CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4, 30 wt% of ZnFe2O4 was well dispersed in 1:1 mixture of ethanol and water and sonicated for 1 h. CdS/C-dot was added to the above mixture and magnetically stirred for another 15 h. The product was washed with ethanol and double-distilled water and dried in an oven at 65 °C.

The preparation method of CdS/C-dot composite used for the preparation of the ternary composite is being given in the supporting information (S1). Pure C-dot was also prepared by the same method without using CdS particles.

For the preparation of the electrodes, the ITO coated glasses were first cut into pieces having approximately 1x1.5 cm2 area. These pieces were then cleaned by sonication in ethanol for around 10–15 min. The contacts between the ITO and copper wire were made by using silver paste and covered by epoxy resin (Araldite). For the deposition of materials on ITO coated glass, an ink of the sample was prepared by dispersing 2 mg of sample in a mixture of 60 µL double-distilled water, 120 µL of ethanol and 20 µL of Nafion and sonicated for around 20 min. The uniform ink was then drop cast on the active area of the photoelectrode and dried in air.

The structural aspect and compositional purity of the fabricated products were confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 advance) using Cu Kα radiation ( λ = 1.5406 Å). The surface morphologies of all the samples were examined by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7800F) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, TECNAI G2, 200 kV). The images of elemental mapping were collected using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) detector. The optical and fluorescence properties of the samples were investigated using UV visible absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV2450) and photoluminescence (PL) spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu). The lifetime measurements of the excited state samples were done using Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC, Fluo Time 300, Picoquant).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Normalisation details for absorption and steady state emission studies
ce:section.ce:para: For the absorption and steady state emission studies the normalisation of the amount of composites and bare material was done by taking the same amount of samples during each measurement. 1 mg of each sample was weighed and dispersed in 1 mL of ethanol by ultrasonication, from which during each measurement 300 µL of the sample solution was taken and diluted with 3 mL of ethanol in the cuvette.

The PEC measurements were carried out at room temperature in a Metrohm PGSTAT204 Autolab workstation using Nova 2.1 software. The source of illumination used was a Xenon arc lamp (300 W) under AM 1.5 G simulated solar irradiation having a power density of 100 mW/cm2. An aqueous solution containing Na2S (0.25 M) and Na2SO3 (0.35 M) (pH = 13) was used as the electrolyte for carrying out the measurements. The PEC measurements were carried out in a three-electrode configuration in a single compartment Teflon cell with a quartz window. The three-electrode configuration consisted of material coated ITO glass as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) studies under dark and light illuminated conditions were carried out in the potential range of −1.0 to 0 V versus Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed at a potential of 0.1 V versus Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) with an AC voltage in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 105 Hz under both dark and illuminated conditions. The Mott-Schottky measurements were carried out under dark conditions at a frequency of 1 kHz within a potential range of −1 to 1 V versus Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl).

The compositional and purity analysis of the synthesized materials were done by powder X-ray diffraction. Fig. 1 displays the XRD pattern of bare CdS and the composites CdS/ZnFe2O4 and CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4.

The XRD pattern of bare CdS shows diffraction peaks at 2 θ values of 25.2°, 26.5 °, 27.9 °, 43.9°, 47.5 ° and 52.0 °. These peaks were assigned to (100), (002), (101), (110), (103) and (112) planes of CdS with hexagonal phase structure matching with JCPDS file no.77–2306. For bare ZnFe2O4, peaks at 18.4 °, 30.2 °, 35.6 °, 37.2 °, 43.3 °, 53.6 °, 57.2 °, 62.8 °, 71.3 ° and 74.3° correspond to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620) and (533) planes of ZnFe2O4 with cubic phase and JCPDS file no. 73–1963 (Fig. S1 in supporting information). The XRD pattern of CdS/C-dot consists of all the characteristic peaks of CdS but no peak for carbon was observed which can be attributed to the amorphous nature of the carbon dot layer (Fig. S2). Further the XRD pattern of the composite of CdS and ZnFe2O4 consists of the characteristic peaks of both CdS and ZnFe2O4. The ternary composite consists of the characteristic peaks of both CdS and ZnFe2O4 only, due to the same reason stated for the binary composite (CdS/C-dot).
The morphology and size of the bare and composite materials were obtained from the Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) as shown in Fig. 2 .

The FESEM images reveal that the bare CdS particles are formed uniformly with size varying between 350±50 nm (Fig. 2a). The HRTEM size distribution bar diagram is given in the supplementary information (Fig. S3). The ZnFe2O4 particles obtained were very small 4–5 nm non uniform particles (Fig. S4). In the binary composite, the CdS particles are covered uniformly by the ZnFe2O4 nano particles (Fig. 2b) and the ternary composite consists of CdS/C-dot layer being surrounded by ZnFe2O4 particles as can be seen in Fig. 2c and in a more magnified image Fig. 2d.

High resolution Transmission Electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to get size, morphology and information regarding the interfaces in the composites. Fig. 3 (a) shows a TEM micrograph of the overall ternary composite at a lower magnification which shows uniform CdS nanospheres being covered by a layer of carbon dots and ZnFe2O4 particles. Further the formation of the ternary composite of CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 was confirmed by the fringes obtained for the d-spacing values of 0.35 nm and 0.25 nm corresponding to the (100) (JCPDS No.77–2306) and (311) (JCPDS no.73–1963) planes of CdS and ZnFe2O4 respectively as shown in Fig. 3(b). But the amorphous carbon dot layer was not recognised in the TEM micrograph as it is being covered by ZnFe2O4 particles. However, the amorphous carbon layer is visible in the binary composite CdS/C-dot as shown in Fig. S5. The carbon dot layer formed over CdS particles is of approximately 4 nm width which confirms the presence of the same layer in ternary composite as well. The TEM micrographs of bare carbon dots are given in the supporting information (Fig. S6).

Further to support the formation of carbon dots we have done the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) mapping in the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mode using a holey carbon grid (Fig. S7). As an additional support we did the C 1s XPS for both bare carbon dots and the ternary composite which confirms the presence of carbon dots. Fig. S8(a and b) shows C 1s spectra of both bare carbon dots and the ternary composite CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4.
Also, the Raman spectrum of the ternary composite in the region of 1325 cm−1 to 1620 cm−1 has been shown in Fig. S9 to confirm the presence of D and G bands of carbon dots.

The composition of the CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 composite was further confirmed by the elemental analysis using the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The FESEM image chosen for mapping is shown in Fig. 4 (a). The mapping images in Fig. 4(b – g) shows the presence of Cd, S, C, Zn, Fe and O elements in the ternary composite respectively. Fig. 4(h) shows the overlay of all the elements. Thus, the elemental mapping images confirm the formation CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 ternary composite with a uniform distribution of all the elements.
The optical absorption characteristics were investigated by using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. Fig. 5 shows the UV–visible absorption spectrum for the bare CdS and its composites.

For pristine CdS, a broad absorption peak appears at around 510 nm which is equivalent to an energy bandgap value of 2.43 eV [42,45]. For the binary composite CdS/ZnFe2O4 along with the peak at 510 nm, there is a shoulder at around 620 nm which corresponds to the energy bandgap value of 2.0 eV close to that for ZnFe2O4 [46]. Further with the incorporation of carbon dot there is a red shift at the optical band edge of CdS which is shifted to 530 nm.This shift reveals that there may be some synergistic interaction between carbon dots and CdS [47]. In the presence of carbon dots a red shift in the absorption maxima of the CdS supraparticles is observed which is attributed to the ability of carbon based nanomaterials to absorb light. An electronic interaction is established at the interface between CdS and carbon dots as a result of which the band gap of the composite is decreased.

Moreover the absorption is also most prominent in the case of the ternary composite. Hence, the overall results reveal that the carbon dot layer plays a very significant role in improving the absorption of visible light [48]. This in turn is expected to help in enhancing the photoelectrochemical activity [49].

The steady state photoluminescence study is very important in order to understand the fate of the electron-hole pairs generated in a semiconductor [50]. The PL studies were carried out at three different excitation wavelengths i.e. 330 nm (Fig. S10a), 360 nm (Fig. S10b) and 400 nm. Fig. 6 depicts the PL spectra of the bare and composite materials recorded at 400 nm. The bare material shows an emission maxima at 498 nm. There is a gradual red shift in the emission maxima from 498 nm to 502 nm for the binary composite and to 520 nm for the ternary composite which reveals that composite formation is taking place.The large red shift in the case of ternary composite indicates that there may be some electronic interaction occuring between carbon dot and CdS [48].

The highest intensity of PL in the case of bare CdS indicates the rapid recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in case of CdS. PL intensity of the binary composite CdS/ZnFe2O4 is considerably lower than the bare one indicating a low charge carrier recombination in case of the binary composite. The highly quenched PL intensity in the case of ternary composite indicates that the charge carriers are well separated from each other. The significantly quenched intensity oberved in the case of the ternary system at all the excitation wavelengths can be attributed to the electron transfer from ZnFe2O4 to CdS via the carbon dot layer and hole transfer in the opposite direction.

Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay dynamics were investigated for bare, binary and ternary composite. The decay profiles were monitored at an emission wavelength 505 nm with an excitation wavelength 405 nm. Fig. 7 shows the TRPL decay curves for the bare and composite materials.

For quantitative assesment of the obtained decay profiles, exponential fitting was carried out. The fitting of the time profiles of the bare and composite materials could not be done with a single exponential function indicating that a complex relaxation process is involved. A bi-exponential function was used for fitting the decay curves [40,51]. The derived average lifetime values obtained are tabulated in Table 1 .

The fitting of the experimental curves has been done with the following equation [52]:
(1)

Where
y 0
is the y-offset of the exponential fit, i.e. the value reached at infinity,
x 0
is the x offset, A is the scaling factor and τ is the decay time constant. The values of the goodness of fit parameter shown in Table 1 show a good fit to the experimental data.

The average PL lifetime of the charge carriers for bare CdS is 0.78 ns and for the binary composite, CdS/ZnFe2O4 is 0.85 ns. The average PL lifetime for the ternary composite, CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 has increased to 2.41 ns as shown in Fig. 7. The average lifetime of charge carriers for CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 is the largest amongst the three, which can be attributed to the formation of the ternary composite heterostructure. The prolonged lifetime of the charge carriers in the case of ternary composite further implied that due to incorporation of the carbon dot layer the interfacial charge transfer improved and hence the charge carriers have to travel longer until they recombine [40,41,53–57]. Also there might be lowering of defect and trap states which usually acts as recombination sites [58].

The steady state and time-resolved photoluminescence decay studies for bare carbon dots and ZnFe2O4 have been given in the supplementary information. (Fig. S11 and Fig. S12). The steady state photoluminescence spectra of bare carbon dots is shown in Fig. S11(a). The pure carbon dots exhibit the excitation wavelength dependent emission characteristics which is most common for fluorescent carbon based materials. With the variation in the excitation wavelength from 300 to 500 nm, the emission spectra also showed a variation from 533 nm to 552 nm. The maximum emission intensity was observed at 535 nm when the excitation wavelength was 450 nm.

The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay dynamics of bare carbon dots was monitored at an emission wavelength of 535 nm with an excitation wavelength 405 nm as shown in Fig. S11(b). The lifetime of the charge carriers for bare carbon dots was obtained after fitting the decay profile bi-exponentially and was found to be 1.15 ns.

Steady state photoluminescence studies for ZnFe2O4 has been done at 400 nm excitation wavelength. The PL spectrum in Fig. S12(a) shows an emission peak which spreads over a large range from 450 nm to about 500 nm. The TRPL decay dynamics of ZnFe2O4 was monitored at an excitation wavelength 405 nm and an emission wavelength of 468 nm. The TRPL decay curve is shown in Fig. S12(b). After a bi-exponential fitting, the lifetime of the charge carriers was found to be 0.42 ns which is much lower than both bare CdS and the composites CdS/ZnFe2O4 and CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4.

On the basis of the reported energy levels of CdS, C-dots and ZnFe2O4 [36,46,59], a possible mechanism is proposed as shown in Scheme 1 . According to the band alignments of the constructed CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 composite, when it is illuminated with a light source, electron-hole pairs are generated in both CdS and ZnFe2O4. The role of carbon dots here is to protect and lower the photocorrosion of CdS supraparticles. Also, it is acting as a photosensitizer by increasing the absorption of the visible light and helping in the transport of the charge carriers across the heterojunctions. Hence the electrons will move from conduction band of ZnFe2O4 to that of CdS with the help of LUMO of carbon dots while holes will move from the valence band of CdS to that of ZnFe2O4 via HOMO of carbon dots.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out both in dark and light conditions in a potential range of −1 to 0 V with respect to the Ag/AgCl reference electrode at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Fig. 8 a shows the LSV plots for all the photoelectrocatalysts. The difference between dark and light current density were calculated for all the systems and the highest difference was observed in the case of the ternary system. The lowest current density was observed in the case of the bare CdS photocatalyst which was also supported by the PL spectra which showed highest intensity and hence maximum electron-hole pair recombination. Table 2 tabulates the values of the current density in both dark and light conditions and also the comparison of photocurrent densities for all the photoelectrocatalysts. The photocurrent density values were determined in the range of −1 to 0 V. However significant difference between the LSV curves was observed after −0.9 V and hence −0.9V was chosen for comparison of photocurrent density values. For the bare CdS the photocurrent density was found to be 0.001 mA/cm2 and for the binary and ternary composite it was found to be 0.009 mA/cm2 and 0.047 mA/cm2 respectively. The photocurrent density for the ternary composite is enhanced nearly 50 times as compared to the bare CdS and 5 times as compared to the binary composite CdS/ZnFe2O4 as incorporation of C-dots in the CdS/ZnFe2O4 composite helps in increasing the overall conductivity and inhibiting the charge carrier recombination.

The applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) for all the materials have been calculated from the corresponding characteristic LSV curves using the following equation [60]:
(2)
ABPE =

Where, I correspond to the photocurrent density obtained at an applied potential |Vapp| and 1.23 V is the standard reversible potential for water splitting. P light is the power density of the light source which is 100 mW/cm2. Fig. 8b shows the comparison of the ABPE values between the bare and composite materials at an applied potential of −0.9 V vs Ag/AgCl. The ABPE values for the bare and binary composites are 0.0003 and 0.0030 % respectively. The ABPE value for the ternary compositre was 0.0155 %,which is approximately 50 and 5 times higher than the bare and binary materials respectively.

EIS measurements were carried out at a potential of 0.1 V w.r.to Ag/AgCl to determine the interfacial resistance, both in the presence and absence of light in all the cases. Nyquist plots for bare CdS and the composites are plotted as shown in Fig. 9
.

The difference in the charge-transfer rates at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface for the photoelectrocatalysts can be observed by the variation in the diameter of the circular arcs which represents the charge transfer resistance, RCT. The largest semicircle diameter was observed in the case of bare photocatalyst in the dark conditions which indicates lowest charge transfer rate due to the fastest charge carrier recombination. Semicircle diameter magnitude in between that of bare and ternary-one was observed for the binary composite CdS/ZnFe2O4 indicating improved charge separation after composite formation. The smallest circular arc diameter in the case of the ternary composite, CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4 in the presence of light indicates lowest charge transfer resistance which suggests that the photogenerated charge carriers are transferred and separated efficiently[4]. The inset in Fig. 9 represents the magnified image of the recorded EIS spectra of the bare and composite materials. The magnified image revealed lowest solution resistance in the case of the ternary composite as well.

Mott-Schottky (M-S) plot can be used to determine the type of a semiconductor, its flat band potential as well as its charge carrier densities[61]. The M-S plot for all the materials is shown in Fig. 10. The positive slope in the case of bare CdS confirms its n-type behaviour i.e. the Fermi level of the semiconductor is located below the conduction band. The plots of the binary and ternary composite have positive slopes till 0.75 V and origin of a negative slope starts after 0.75 V which can be due to the p-type behaviour of ZnFe2O4 [4].

The charge carrier densities for all the systems have been calculated using the equations given below[61]:

Where C is the surface charge capacitance, ∊ 0 is the permittivity of vacuum, ∊ is the dielectric constant of the material (8.9) [62], Nd denotes the dopant density, the applied electrode potential is denoted by V, VFB denotes the flat band potential and kT/∊ 0 is the temperature dependent correction term. Table 3 shows the charge carrier density and flat band potential values of the bare and composite catalysts.
The charge carrier density value after the incorporation of carbon dot has improved in comparison to the bare and binary phase due to efficient separation of the charge carriers [4].

We have incorporated a carbon dot layer in between a CdS/ZnFe2O4 Type II composite heterostructure and prepared CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4. The role of C-dots is in ensuring facile charge transfer between CdS and ZnFe2O4 because of its good electrical conductivity. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) study shows that maximum PL intensity is observed in the case of bare CdS while minimum intensity in the case of ternary composite after the incorporation of carbon dot layer. The PL spectra clearly reveals that maximum charge carrier recombination occurs in bare materials while minimum in ternary composite. Further, the time-resolved photoluminescence decay dynamics have been studied. The lifetime of the charge carriers in the case of ternary composite was found to be 2.41 ns, which is much higher than the bare (0.78 ns) and binary composite (0.85 ns), indicating the improved charge separation and the stability after composite formation (CdS/C-dot/ZnFe2O4). Eventually, this aids in the enhancement of the photocurrent generation. The ternary composite produced around 50 and 5 times higher photocurrent density than bare and binary composite materials respectively.
